manifest.title: U.S.S. Aster Vale New Orleans-Class Ship Dataset

areas.name: Bridge
areas.zone: Deck 1 Galaxy-family bridge module scaled to frigate size
hardFacts: The bridge uses familiar Starfleet formality at smaller frigate scale.
constraints: Bridge scenes should turn what the ship sees into what Starfleet will do about it.

areas.name: Ready room and observation room
hardFacts: The observation room is formal enough for senior-staff synthesis but smaller than Galaxy-class ceremony.
constraints: Discovered facts here should become accountable records, not detached exposition.

areas.name: Wardroom, mess, and diplomatic cabin
areas.zone: Forward and mid-saucer social and diplomatic spaces
hardFacts: The wardroom carries compact Starfleet dignity without flagship scale.
constraints: Social space should show how visibility and records affect people, not only policy.

areas.name: Sickbay
hardFacts: Medical truth can protect or endanger a person when records are politically contested.
constraints: Medical logs may require sealing, redaction, or command-level protection.

areas.name: Transporter rooms, security, and brig
hardFacts: Transporter movement can become asylum, kidnapping, rescue, extradition refusal, or evidence contamination.
constraints: Movement must remain legally and ethically legible.

areas.name: Mission ops and pod-control room
areas.zone: Central mission space linked to dorsal and ventral pod access trunks
areas.exteriorPlacement: Internal pod-control space tied to two dorsal saucer pods and one ventral secondary-hull pod
hardFacts: New Orleans-class ships carry two dorsal saucer mission/sensor pods and one ventral secondary-hull pod in Directive baseline.
Mission pods are specialized perception systems, not routine shuttlebays or infinite cargo modules by default.
constraints: Pod data has arc, power, calibration, maintenance, and custody limits.

areas.name: Science labs and pod access
areas.zone: Saucer labs with access trunks to dorsal pods
areas.exteriorPlacement: Internal access toward dorsal saucer mission/sensor pods
hardFacts: Dorsal pod access is specialized and constrained, not a normal hallway network.
constraints: Pod calibration can delay action even when command wants immediate certainty.

areas.name: Main engineering and deflector control
areas.zone: Compact secondary hull and lower mission support
areas.exteriorPlacement: Secondary hull power and deflector support
hardFacts: Pod operation competes with shields, transporters, warp, and other ship systems through a finite power grid.
constraints: Age is not incompetence; the ship is proven, repairable, and still bounded by power tradeoffs.

areas.name: Cargo and field stores
areas.zone: Lower saucer or upper secondary service volume
hardFacts: Cargo turns records, charts, and evidence into physical objects with custody.
constraints: Which object is sealed, returned, or moved should matter.

areas.name: Aft saucer/neck shuttlebay
areas.exteriorPlacement: Aft saucer/neck service bay approached from astern and slightly above the engineering hull
hardFacts: Directive New Orleans baseline places the primary small-craft bay in the aft saucer/neck service zone.
Shuttles approach from astern and slightly above the engineering hull.
Mission pods are not routine shuttlebays by default.
constraints: Do not depict shuttles routinely entering a mission pod.
Do not scale the bay into a massive Galaxy-class dorsal flight deck.

areas.name: Lower support and ventral pod access
areas.zone: Lower hull, environmental, maintenance, and ventral pod access
areas.exteriorPlacement: Lower secondary-hull support near the ventral mission/sensor pod
hardFacts: The ventral pod's reliability depends on lower support access, seals, relays, and calibration.
constraints: Remote diagnostics may not replace physical pod access under sabotage or damage.

systems.name: Mission/sensor pod suite
systems.scope: Two dorsal saucer pods and one ventral secondary-hull pod
systems.capability: Specialized sensor, survey, tactical-support, or mission-package functions that make hidden space visible.

systems.name: Records, charts, and evidence custody
systems.scope: Ship logs, chart authority, medical/security records, and evidence handling
systems.capability: Controls what the ship knows, what it logs, who can read it, and when it becomes public.

systems.name: Frigate tactical systems
systems.scope: Phaser arrays, photon torpedoes, shields, and patrol authority
systems.capability: Credible patrol and escort capability without task-group dominance.

systems.name: Older-but-proven power grid
systems.scope: Mature Galaxy-era power distribution adapted for frigate pods and frontier refits
systems.capability: Understood and repairable systems that support pod loads, shields, transporters, and warp with real tradeoffs.

systems.name: Small-craft movement and custody
systems.scope: Aft shuttlebay, passenger screening, pod-safe approach windows, and movement logs
systems.capability: Moves teams, witnesses, casualties, and supplies while preserving legal and operational records.

payload.summary: U.S.S. Aster Vale is a New Orleans-class vessel. A New Orleans-class ship is a mature Starfleet frigate: smaller than Galaxy-class grandeur, formal enough for diplomacy, and defined by mission/sensor pods that make visibility, evidence, and chart custody consequential. Campaign use: U.S.S. Aster Vale: Lacuna March frontier patrol, chart validation, protected visibility, evidence custody, asylum, and modular mission-pod work.
constraints: Do not treat the ship as a full Galaxy-class city.
Do not ignore the pods or use them as routine shuttlebays by default.
hardAnchors: Galaxy-family silhouette at frigate scale.
Two dorsal saucer mission/sensor pods and one ventral secondary-hull pod in Directive baseline.
Primary small-craft bay is in the aft saucer/neck service zone, approached from astern.

payload.summary: The exterior reads as a compact Galaxy-era frigate with a familiar saucer, compact engineering hull, nacelles, and three prominent mission/sensor pods.
constraints: Exterior prose should keep pod placement, saucer scale, engineering hull, and aft bay geometry consistent.
hardAnchors: The three pods are class-defining exterior features.
The class is significantly smaller than a Galaxy-class ship.
Pod arcs and calibration can affect what the ship can prove.

payload.summary: The aft saucer/neck shuttlebay turns passage into physical movement and official record. Directive New Orleans baseline places the primary small-craft bay in the aft saucer/neck service zone. Shuttles approach from astern and slightly above the engineering hull. Mission pods are not routine shuttlebays by default.
constraints: Do not depict shuttles routinely entering a mission pod.
Do not scale the bay into a massive Galaxy-class dorsal flight deck.
hardAnchors: Directive New Orleans baseline places the primary small-craft bay in the aft saucer/neck service zone.
Shuttles approach from astern and slightly above the engineering hull.
Mission pods are not routine shuttlebays by default.
avoid: Do not depict shuttles routinely entering a mission pod.
Do not scale the bay into a massive Galaxy-class dorsal flight deck.

payload.summary: The New Orleans bridge turns pod returns, border contacts, and patrol reports into accountable command action.
constraints: Bridge scenes should turn what the ship sees into what Starfleet will do about it.
hardAnchors: The bridge uses familiar Starfleet formality at smaller frigate scale.

refs: 5.2 Ready room and observation room, Deck 2

payload.summary: Command support rooms turn discovered facts into records the ship and commander must own.
constraints: Discovered facts here should become accountable records, not detached exposition.
hardAnchors: The observation room is formal enough for senior-staff synthesis but smaller than Galaxy-class ceremony.

refs: 5.3 Wardroom, mess, and diplomatic cabin, Decks 3-5

payload.summary: The wardroom turns formal Starfleet procedure into personal trust, asylum pressure, and crew fatigue.
constraints: Social space should show how visibility and records affect people, not only policy.
hardAnchors: The wardroom carries compact Starfleet dignity without flagship scale.

payload.summary: Sickbay turns disputed facts into bodies, medical records, and privacy risk.
constraints: Medical logs may require sealing, redaction, or command-level protection.
hardAnchors: Medical truth can protect or endanger a person when records are politically contested.

refs: 5.5 Transporter rooms, security, and brig, Deck 6

payload.summary: Transporter and security spaces turn movement into custody, protection, and legal risk.
constraints: Movement must remain legally and ethically legible.
hardAnchors: Transporter movement can become asylum, kidnapping, rescue, extradition refusal, or evidence contamination.

refs: 5.6 Mission ops and pod-control room, Deck 9

payload.summary: Mission ops is where the New Orleans turns perception into institutional risk.
constraints: Pod data has arc, power, calibration, maintenance, and custody limits.
hardAnchors: New Orleans-class ships carry two dorsal saucer mission/sensor pods and one ventral secondary-hull pod in Directive baseline.
Mission pods are specialized perception systems, not routine shuttlebays or infinite cargo modules by default.

refs: 5.7 Science labs and pod access, Decks 7-8

payload.summary: Science labs test whether seeing is knowing and whether pod data can be trusted.
constraints: Pod calibration can delay action even when command wants immediate certainty.
hardAnchors: Dorsal pod access is specialized and constrained, not a normal hallway network.

refs: 5.8 Main engineering and deflector control, Decks 10-11

payload.summary: Engineering turns pod loadouts and institutional intent into power, maintenance, and repair cost.
constraints: Age is not incompetence; the ship is proven, repairable, and still bounded by power tradeoffs.
hardAnchors: Pod operation competes with shields, transporters, warp, and other ship systems through a finite power grid.

payload.summary: Cargo makes the ship act on what it records: probes, relief, evidence, and field gear.
constraints: Which object is sealed, returned, or moved should matter.
hardAnchors: Cargo turns records, charts, and evidence into physical objects with custody.

payload.summary: The aft saucer/neck shuttlebay turns passage into physical movement and official record.
constraints: Do not depict shuttles routinely entering a mission pod.
Do not scale the bay into a massive Galaxy-class dorsal flight deck.
hardAnchors: Directive New Orleans baseline places the primary small-craft bay in the aft saucer/neck service zone.
Shuttles approach from astern and slightly above the engineering hull.
Mission pods are not routine shuttlebays by default.

cards.title: Lower support and ventral pod access

refs: 5.11 Lower support and ventral pod access, Deck 14

payload.summary: Lower support makes the class signature pods physically maintainable and vulnerable.
constraints: Remote diagnostics may not replace physical pod access under sabotage or damage.
hardAnchors: The ventral pod's reliability depends on lower support access, seals, relays, and calibration.

payload.summary: Mission pods extend perception and make visibility consequential.
constraints: Capability: Specialized sensor, survey, tactical-support, or mission-package functions that make hidden space visible.
Dependencies: power allocation; calibration; pod arc; maintenance access; data custody.
Failure modes: blind spot; false certainty; power conflict; sealed-data exposure; pod damage.
hardAnchors: Two dorsal saucer pods and one ventral secondary-hull pod
Specialized sensor, survey, tactical-support, or mission-package functions that make hidden space visible.

payload.summary: Records are story objects: they protect, expose, authorize, and haunt.
constraints: Capability: Controls what the ship knows, what it logs, who can read it, and when it becomes public.
Dependencies: access controls; raw data archives; command authorization; medical privacy; security chain of custody.
Failure modes: exposure of protected location; evidence contamination; sealed-data leak; public-trust collapse.
hardAnchors: Ship logs, chart authority, medical/security records, and evidence handling
Controls what the ship knows, what it logs, who can read it, and when it becomes public.

payload.summary: Tactical authority is bounded by patrol scale, legitimacy, and what the ship can prove.
constraints: Capability: Credible patrol and escort capability without task-group dominance.
Dependencies: weapon arcs; shield sectors; rules of engagement; pod power demand; civilian proximity.
Failure modes: arc limitation; escalation; inventory pressure; shield compromise.
hardAnchors: Phaser arrays, photon torpedoes, shields, and patrol authority
Credible patrol and escort capability without task-group dominance.

payload.summary: The ship is proven, not shabby: age means repairable knowledge and inherited constraints.
constraints: Capability: Understood and repairable systems that support pod loads, shields, transporters, and warp with real tradeoffs.
Dependencies: EPS distribution; pod loadout; warp demand; shield status; refit history.
Failure modes: pod power conflict; old conduit stress; calibration drift; temporary blind spot.
hardAnchors: Mature Galaxy-era power distribution adapted for frigate pods and frontier refits
Understood and repairable systems that support pod loads, shields, transporters, and warp with real tradeoffs.

payload.summary: Small-craft movement aboard a New Orleans is physical passage and official record at the same time.
constraints: Capability: Moves teams, witnesses, casualties, and supplies while preserving legal and operational records.
Dependencies: bay clearance; pod sweep timing; screening logs; security routing; medical/cargo readiness.
Failure modes: unlogged arrival; pod interference; screening failure; custody ambiguity.
hardAnchors: Aft shuttlebay, passenger screening, pod-safe approach windows, and movement logs
Moves teams, witnesses, casualties, and supplies while preserving legal and operational records.
